stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks gestation with known uterine fibroids (largest 8 cm anterior fundal) presents with acute onset severe right upper quadrant and epigastric pain, vomiting, and localised tenderness. She is afebrile. Ultrasound shows a 9 cm fibroid with heterogeneous echotexture and no free fluid. The foetus is active with normal growth and Dopplers. What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate management?
lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate management?

options.text: Red degeneration of fibroid; manage with analgesia (paracetamol, codeine if needed), rest, and monitor foetal wellbeing
options.rationale: Red (carneous) degeneration of a uterine fibroid occurs in the second and third trimesters due to outgrowth of blood supply, causing acute pain, vomiting, and tenderness. Management is conservative with analgesia, hydration, and foetal monitoring. Surgery is rarely needed. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)

options.text: Placental abruption; immediate delivery by caesarean section
options.rationale: Abruption causes uterine tenderness and pain but usually with vaginal bleeding and foetal distress, and USS would show retroplacental clot. The fibroid appearance is classic for degeneration. (Source: RANZCOG: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Ovarian torsion; emergency laparoscopy
options.rationale: The pain is uterine/fundal and ultrasound shows a fibroid, not an ovarian mass. (Source: RANZCOG: Adnexal Masses in Pregnancy)

options.text: Acute cholecystitis; abdominal ultrasound with gallbladder assessment
options.rationale: RUQ pain could be cholecystitis, but the known fibroid and ultrasound showing heterogeneous fibroid point to red degeneration. (Source: RANZCOG: Fibroids in Pregnancy)

options.text: Preterm labour; tocolysis with nifedipine and betamethasone
options.rationale: There is no evidence of contractions or cervical change; the pain is localised, not labour. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm Labour)

stem: A 24-year-old G2P1 at 29+4 weeks gestation presents with a sudden gush of clear fluid and ongoing trickling. She is afebrile and has no contractions. Speculum examination confirms pooling of clear fluid with positive nitrazine test. Cervix is closed and long. CTG is reassuring. She received a course of betamethasone at 27 weeks for a threatened preterm labour that resolved. What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen for prolonging latency and reducing neonatal morbidity?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days
options.rationale: The ORACLE I trial demonstrated that erythromycin prolongs pregnancy and reduces neonatal morbidity in PPROM. Co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased risk of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis. A single rescue course of betamethasone should also be given as the prior course was >7 days ago. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline; ORACLE I Trial)

options.text: IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily for 48 hours, then oral amoxicillin for 5 days
options.rationale: This regimen is used for chorioamnionitis or established infection, not for latency in uncomplicated PPROM. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is associated with an increased risk of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis and should be avoided in PPROM. (Source: ORACLE I Trial)

options.text: No antibiotics are required as she is afebrile
options.rationale: Antibiotics are recommended in PPROM to prolong latency and reduce chorioamnionitis and neonatal morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 1 g daily for 7 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone is not the standard regimen for PPROM latency; erythromycin is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: PPROM Guideline)

stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 30+2 weeks gestation with known chronic hypertension presents with worsening epigastric pain, nausea, and headache. Her blood pressure is 160/105 mmHg despite oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein. Laboratory results reveal: haemoglobin 105 g/L, platelets 55 ×10⁹/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 280 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 240 U/L, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 650 U/L, haptoglobin <0.3 g/L, serum creatinine 115 µmol/L, urate 0.52 mmol/L. Peripheral blood film shows schistocytes. CTG shows a baseline of 150 bpm with reduced variability and occasional late decelerations. She has received the first dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM 6 hours ago. Intravenous hydralazine has been given for blood pressure control. The obstetric and anaesthetic teams are at the bedside. What is the most appropriate plan regarding delivery?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan regarding delivery?

options.text: Administer the second dose of betamethasone at 24 hours and aim for delivery at 48 hours from the first dose, provided maternal and foetal conditions remain stable
options.rationale: This woman has HELLP syndrome with severe features: platelets <100 ×10⁹/L, AST >70 U/L, LDH >600 U/L, and evidence of haemolysis (low haptoglobin, schistocytes). Delivery is indicated within 24 hours, not delayed for completion of the steroid course. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines: Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy)

options.text: Proceed to delivery within 24 hours, preferably after administering the second dose of betamethasone at 12 hours from the first if maternal and foetal status permit, but do not delay beyond 24 hours
options.rationale: HELLP syndrome is a progressive condition that can deteriorate rapidly. Delivery is the definitive treatment. While completing the steroid course is ideal, in the presence of worsening features (platelets dropping, rising transaminases, renal impairment, foetal compromise on CTG), delivery should not be postponed beyond 24 hours. A second dose of betamethasone can be given at 12–24 hours if feasible, but not at the expense of maternal safety. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines; RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Give the second dose of betamethasone now (6 hours after first) and deliver immediately after the infusion, as expedited delivery is required regardless of steroid status
options.rationale: The recommended interval between betamethasone doses is 24 hours, though a minimal interval of 12 hours is sometimes considered. Giving the second dose at 6 hours does not optimise neonatal benefit. Delivery should be expedited but does not need to be immediate if the mother is stabilised and foetal monitoring is continuous; aiming for 12–24 hours is acceptable. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Commence magnesium sulphate infusion for foetal neuroprotection and expectantly manage for 48 hours to complete the steroid course, as long as platelets remain above 50 ×10⁹/L
options.rationale: Expectant management beyond 24 hours in HELLP syndrome with foetal compromise is not recommended due to high risk of eclampsia, hepatic rupture, and abruptio placentae. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Perform immediate caesarean delivery under general anaesthesia without waiting for the second betamethasone dose
options.rationale: Immediate delivery is indicated if there is acute deterioration, eclampsia, or non-reassuring foetal status that does not respond to intrauterine resuscitation. With current CTG abnormalities, close monitoring and expedited delivery within 12–24 hours is reasonable, but not emergent at this moment. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks gestation with severe pre-eclampsia is being managed with intravenous magnesium sulphate (4 g loading dose, then 1 g/hour) and oral labetalol 200 mg BD. Four hours after commencing the magnesium infusion, she has a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes. The seizure self-terminates. On examination, she is postictal with a GCS of 10. Blood pressure is 150/100 mmHg, respiratory rate 16, patellar reflexes present but reduced. The magnesium level 1 hour ago was 1.8 mmol/L (therapeutic range 1.7–3.5 mmol/L). Urine output has been 30 mL/hr. The foetal heart rate is 130 bpm with normal variability. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Administer an additional 2 g intravenous bolus of magnesium sulphate over 5–10 minutes and continue the maintenance infusion at 1 g/hour
options.rationale: A recurrent eclamptic seizure while on magnesium therapy (therapeutic level) indicates a need for an additional bolus of 2 g over 5–10 minutes, with close monitoring for magnesium toxicity. A further bolus of 2 g may be repeated if seizures persist. Intubation and phenytoin are reserved for refractory cases. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines: Eclampsia; KEMH Eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Stop the magnesium sulphate infusion immediately, administer intravenous calcium gluconate 1 g for magnesium toxicity, and commence intravenous phenytoin 15 mg/kg loading dose
options.rationale: The magnesium level is within the therapeutic range, not toxic. Calcium gluconate is indicated for magnesium toxicity (absent reflexes, respiratory depression). Phenytoin is less effective than magnesium for eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Give intravenous midazolam 5 mg to terminate the seizure and arrange an urgent non-contrast CT head to exclude intracranial haemorrhage
options.rationale: While CT head may be indicated if there are focal neurological signs or atypical presentation, the immediate management of recurrent eclamptic seizure is an additional magnesium bolus, not benzodiazepine. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Prepare for immediate caesarean delivery, as recurrent seizures despite magnesium indicate impending foetal demise
options.rationale: Delivery is the definitive treatment for pre-eclampsia/eclampsia, but the immediate priority is maternal stabilisation and seizure control. Delivery can be expedited once she is stable. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

options.text: Administer intravenous labetalol 20 mg to urgently lower blood pressure to below 140/90 mmHg, as hypertension is the trigger for seizure recurrence
options.rationale: While blood pressure control is important, the immediate priority is to stop the seizure with additional magnesium, not antihypertensives. (Source: SOMANZ Guideline)

stem: A 31-year-old primigravida at 30+6 weeks with early-onset foetal growth restriction (estimated foetal weight below the 3rd centile) has been managed as an inpatient for the past week. Today's umbilical artery Doppler shows absent end-diastolic flow (AEDF), middle cerebral artery Doppler shows a low pulsatility index (brain-sparing), and ductus venosus Doppler shows a positive a-wave. Amniotic fluid index is 6.5 cm. CTG demonstrates a baseline of 145 bpm, reduced variability (4 bpm), and occasional spontaneous accelerations, but no decelerations. She received a full course of betamethasone (two doses 24 hours apart) 8 days ago. What is the most appropriate timing for delivery?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing for delivery?

options.text: Administer a rescue course of betamethasone and deliver within 24 hours, given AEDF and reduced CTG variability
options.rationale: A rescue course is indicated if delivery is anticipated within 7 days and the prior course was >7 days ago. However, with a positive DV a-wave and no CTG decelerations, delivery can be planned within 24–48 hours after the rescue course, not immediately. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

options.text: Deliver within 24–48 hours, giving a rescue course of betamethasone as the prior course was 8 days ago, and commence magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability but positive DV a-wave and no decelerations indicates delivery within 24–48 hours. A rescue steroid course is indicated because >7 days have passed and delivery is anticipated. Magnesium sulphate is given for neuroprotection (<32 weeks). (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH FGR Guideline)

options.text: Immediate caesarean delivery within 2 hours, as AEDF with reduced variability indicates acute foetal decompensation
options.rationale: Immediate delivery is for reversed EDF, abnormal DV (absent/reversed a-wave), or recurrent CTG decelerations. This foetus has a positive DV a-wave and no decelerations, allowing 24–48 hours. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Continue expectant management with daily CTG and repeat Dopplers in 72 hours, as a positive DV a-wave is reassuring
options.rationale: AEDF with reduced CTG variability is an indication for delivery, not continued observation. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Deliver at 34 weeks once the rescue steroid course is effective, as AEDF alone is not an indication for preterm delivery
options.rationale: AEDF at 30 weeks is an indication for delivery within 24–48 hours; waiting until 34 weeks would risk foetal death. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 39+2 weeks gestation is in spontaneous labour with a prolonged second stage and meconium-stained liquor. The membranes ruptured 5 hours ago. She suddenly becomes agitated, complains of difficulty breathing, and then collapses with loss of consciousness. The cardiac monitor shows pulseless electrical activity. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation is commenced immediately, and an emergency caesarean section is performed in the labour ward 6 minutes after the arrest. A live infant is delivered with Apgars of 3, 5, and 7. Following delivery, the mother has return of spontaneous circulation but is noted to have diffuse oozing from the surgical wound, venipuncture sites, and the uterus is atonic with heavy bleeding. Laboratory investigations drawn 20 minutes after arrest show: haemoglobin 62 g/L, platelets 18 ×10⁹/L, fibrinogen 0.4 g/L, activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) >120 seconds, prothrombin time (PT) >60 seconds, D-dimer >100,000 ng/mL. A massive transfusion protocol has been activated and the patient is receiving packed red blood cells and fresh frozen plasma. She has received 4 units of FFP and 6 units of RBCs, but continues to bleed profusely. Her repeat fibrinogen level is 0.6 g/L. What is the most critical blood product to administer next to improve haemostasis?
lead_in: What is the most critical blood product to administer next?

options.text: Cryoprecipitate 10 units (or fibrinogen concentrate 4 g) to raise the fibrinogen level above 2.0 g/L
options.rationale: Amniotic fluid embolism causes a consumptive coagulopathy with severe hypofibrinogenaemia. Fibrinogen is the first clotting factor to reach critically low levels. Guidelines recommend maintaining fibrinogen >2.0 g/L during obstetric haemorrhage; cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate is the most efficient way to achieve this. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism; SOMANZ/ANZCA Guidelines for Massive Transfusion)

options.text: Pooled platelets 1 adult dose to correct the severe thrombocytopenia
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is indicated when the platelet count is <50 ×10⁹/L in the context of active bleeding, but the immediate life-threatening defect is the extremely low fibrinogen, which must be corrected first to stabilise clot formation. (Source: ANZCA Massive Transfusion Guidelines)

options.text: More fresh frozen plasma 15 mL/kg to replace all clotting factors simultaneously
options.rationale: FFP contains fibrinogen but at low concentration; large volumes would be needed to raise fibrinogen adequately, risking volume overload. Cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate provides a concentrated source. (Source: RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Prothrombinex-HT 25 IU/kg to reverse the elevated INR and APTT
options.rationale: Prothrombinex concentrates factors II, IX, and X but contains negligible fibrinogen. It is not the priority when fibrinogen is <1.0 g/L. (Source: ANZCA Massive Transfusion Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g bolus followed by infusion, as hyperfibrinolysis is the dominant pathology
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an adjunct in PPH but does not replace the depleted fibrinogen. Severe hypofibrinogenaemia requires replacement, not just antifibrinolytics. (Source: WOMAN Trial; RANZCOG: Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

stem: A 36-year-old G5P3 with two previous lower-segment caesarean sections and one first-trimester curettage has a known anterior major placenta praevia. Antenatal ultrasound and MRI suggest placenta accreta without frank percreta. She has expressed a strong desire for future fertility. At 36+0 weeks, she experiences a self-limited bleed and an elective caesarean is performed at 37+0 weeks. The surgical team includes a gynaecological oncologist. At delivery, a high transverse uterine incision is made above the placental edge. After delivery of the infant, the placenta does not separate spontaneously. Gentle controlled cord traction is unsuccessful, and the myometrium over the lower segment is noted to be thin and vascular with the placenta invading the uterine wall. Attempts at manual removal cause torrential haemorrhage of approximately 1.5 L in 3 minutes. The uterus is otherwise well-contracted, and the upper segment is normal. The patient remains haemodynamically stable with aggressive volume replacement and blood products. She reiterates her desire for uterine conservation if possible. What is the most appropriate surgical approach?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate surgical approach?

options.text: Leave the placenta in situ, close the uterus, and consider postoperative methotrexate or uterine artery embolisation, with delayed hysterectomy if needed
options.rationale: For a woman with placenta accreta who desires future fertility, leaving the placenta in situ is a recognised conservative option. Postoperative methotrexate may accelerate placental involution. Hysterectomy is the definitive treatment but sacrifices fertility. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum; FIGO Consensus Guidelines on PAS)

options.text: Proceed immediately to total abdominal hysterectomy with the placenta left in situ, as this is the safest management for accreta
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is the standard management but does not preserve fertility. In a patient requesting conservation, leaving the placenta in situ is a valid alternative if the bleeding is controlled. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS Guideline)

options.text: Perform a uterine compression suture (B-Lynch) and uterine artery ligation to control bleeding, then close
options.rationale: Compression sutures and vessel ligation are useful for atonic PPH, but with placenta accreta, the bleeding is from the placental bed invasion, and these measures are unlikely to control it without removing the placenta. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS Guideline)

options.text: Attempt to remove the placenta piecemeal with sharp dissection and oversew the bleeding sites
options.rationale: Piecemeal removal risks incomplete evacuation, ongoing haemorrhage, and uterine perforation. Leaving the placenta in situ is safer for conservation. (Source: FIGO PAS Guidelines)

options.text: Perform a caesarean hysterectomy but preserve the ovaries and refer for surrogacy
options.rationale: This does not preserve fertility; the patient desires uterine conservation. (Source: RANZCOG: PAS Guideline)

stem: A 29-year-old primigravida with early-onset foetal growth restriction (estimated foetal weight <3rd centile) is being monitored as an inpatient at 29+3 weeks. Serial ultrasound today shows new reversed end-diastolic flow (REDF) in the umbilical artery. Previous Dopplers 3 days ago showed absent end-diastolic flow. Ductus venosus Doppler shows a positive a-wave. Amniotic fluid index is 5.2 cm (oligohydramnios). CTG shows a baseline of 140 bpm, reduced variability (3–5 bpm), and no accelerations, but no decelerations. She received a full course of betamethasone 10 days ago. She is on oral labetalol for chronic hypertension. On examination, blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, no proteinuria. The neonatal team has been informed and is ready. What is the most appropriate timing of delivery?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing of delivery?

options.text: Deliver within 24 hours, administering a rescue course of betamethasone as the prior course was over 7 days ago, and commence magnesium sulphate for foetal neuroprotection
options.rationale: REDF is a stronger indication for delivery than AEDF, but with a positive DV a-wave and no CTG decelerations, delivery within 24–48 hours is still acceptable, allowing time for rescue steroids. However, REDF warrants more urgency than AEDF; guidelines often recommend delivery within 24 hours. The rescue course should be given if >7 days have passed, but the priority is delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline)

options.text: Deliver within 24 hours, as REDF with oligohydramnios and reduced CTG variability indicates acute foetal decompensation requiring delivery after a single dose of betamethasone if feasible
options.rationale: REDF, especially with oligohydramnios and a non-reassuring CTG, mandates delivery within 24 hours. While completion of steroids is ideal, maternal and foetal safety take priority. A rescue dose of betamethasone can be given and delivery performed as soon as feasible, ideally within 24 hours. Magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Foetal Growth Restriction Guideline; KEMH FGR Guideline)

options.text: Immediate caesarean delivery within 2 hours, as REDF with reduced variability is a preterminal foetal heart rate pattern that cannot await even a partial steroid course
options.rationale: Immediate delivery is reserved for recurrent decelerations or abnormal ductus venosus (absent/reversed a-wave). With a positive DV a-wave and no decelerations, a short window for steroids is permissible but not >24 hours. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Administer a rescue course of betamethasone over 48 hours and deliver at 48 hours if the CTG remains unchanged
options.rationale: Waiting 48 hours with REDF and oligohydramnios is too long; the risk of foetal death increases significantly. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

options.text: Continue expectant management with daily Dopplers and CTG three times daily, as a positive DV a-wave suggests the foetus is still compensating
options.rationale: REDF is an indication for delivery, not continued observation. (Source: RANZCOG: FGR Guideline)

stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a 3-month history of abdominal bloating and early satiety. She has lost 5 kg unintentionally over the past 3 months. Her mother died of ovarian cancer at age 62. On examination, there is a palpable mass in the right lower quadrant. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals a right adnexal mass measuring 7 cm with irregular solid components, septations, and moderate ascites. Both ovaries are visualised; the left is atrophic. The serum CA-125 level is 320 U/mL. The ultrasound gives a morphology score (U) of 3 (multilocular cyst with solid areas, ascites, and bilateral involvement). She is postmenopausal (M score = 3). What is the calculated Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI), and what is the most appropriate next step?
lead_in: What is the calculated RMI and the most appropriate next step?

options.text: RMI = 2880; refer to a gynaecological oncologist for consideration of surgical staging
options.rationale: RMI = U (3) × M (3) × CA-125 (320) = 2880. An RMI >200 indicates a high risk of malignancy, and referral to a gynaecological oncologist is recommended for surgical staging. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses in Postmenopausal Women; NICE Guideline CG122)

options.text: RMI = 960; repeat CA-125 in 3 months and reassess
options.rationale: The calculation is incorrect; RMI is not 960. With an RMI >200, urgent oncology referral is required, not observation. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses)

options.text: RMI = 2880; schedule a simple oophorectomy by a general gynaecologist
options.rationale: High RMI warrants referral to a gynaecological oncologist for comprehensive surgical staging, not simple oophorectomy alone. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses)

options.text: RMI = 1440; refer for a CT scan, and if negative, discharge to GP
options.rationale: RMI calculation is wrong; and even with a high RMI, CT is part of staging but referral to oncology is mandatory, not discharge. (Source: NICE CG122)

options.text: RMI = 2880; this is a low risk; manage conservatively with repeat ultrasound in 6 weeks
options.rationale: An RMI >200 is high risk, not low; urgent oncology referral is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Ovarian Masses)

stem: A 32-year-old primigravida with gestational diabetes (diet-controlled) and a macrosomic foetus (estimated 4.5 kg) is in the second stage of labour. The foetal head delivers spontaneously, but the chin retracts and the turtle-neck sign appears. McRoberts position and suprapubic pressure are unsuccessful. The accoucheur then attempts delivery of the posterior arm, which is unsuccessful after two attempts because the posterior shoulder is tightly impacted. Internal rotation manoeuvres (Rubin II and Woods' screw) are also unsuccessful. The time from delivery of the head is now 4 minutes. The foetal heart rate is 50 bpm. The woman has an epidural and cannot move easily. The theatre is 5 minutes away. What is the most appropriate next manoeuvre?
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Attempt the Gaskin all-fours manoeuvre, even with the epidural, with assistance to roll the woman onto her hands and knees
options.rationale: When all internal manoeuvres have failed and the foetal head is delivered, the all-fours position (Gaskin manoeuvre) can increase pelvic diameters and is often effective. It can be attempted even with an epidural if assistance is available. Zavanelli (cephalic replacement) is a last resort with high morbidity. Symphysiotomy is considered if all else fails. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia; ALSO Guidelines)

options.text: Perform the Zavanelli manoeuvre (cephalic replacement and emergency caesarean)
options.rationale: Zavanelli is reserved for catastrophic situations where the foetal head cannot be delivered vaginally; it carries high maternal and foetal morbidity and should only be considered after all other measures, including Gaskin, have been attempted. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Apply firm fundal pressure to dislodge the impacted shoulder
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia as it worsens impaction and increases the risk of brachial plexus injury. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform an episiotomy and immediately attempt the McRoberts position again
options.rationale: Episiotomy alone does not resolve bony impaction; further internal manoeuvres or Gaskin are indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Proceed directly to symphysiotomy to increase pelvic dimensions
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a last resort and is rarely performed in Australia; the Gaskin manoeuvre should be attempted first. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder Dystocia)
